comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

1. As a professional writer, do you find the state of the industry depressing? Hundreds of thousands of people aspire to be writers, many of them quite talented, leading to a glut of books on the market, most of which will be immediately forgotten. Even those that make it struggle to capture the interest of an audience and maintain it over the long term, and the pace of work can be unrelenting. Overall, writing seems to be a very precarious and cutthroat profession, similar to the entertainment or music industries. I imagine that you went through a long period in the beginning without a light at the end of the tunnel – how did you manage?

2. Do you have any expectations of leaving behind a legacy with your writing career? Does it make any difference to you whether your books will be read in the far future or end up as curiosities in second-hand book stores?

3. Do you maintain any system for keeping track of all the books that you’ve read, and those that you intend to read?

4. What is your process for deciding to buy a physical copy of a book as opposed to loaning it from the library?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The state of the publishing industry hasn't actually changed much since the invention of the printing press. In Elizabethan England, writers were grumbling about inadequate income, lousy reviews, dishonest publishers, fickle audiences, and did I mention inadequate income? I knew what I was in for when I set out to write professionally, and I assumed that it would take at least a decade of hard work before I started getting any significant income. I had the huge advantage that my wife was able to support us, so I spent that decade as a househusband, combining long hours of writing with cooking, cleaning, etc. At this point I'm supporting us with my writing income in fairly comfortable style, so I can't complain.

2) For 75 years after my death, my copyrights will remain in existence, and I've arranged to have them support some Masonic charities after I'm gone. After that? Anybody's guess. I hope a lot of my books end up in secondhand bookstores, though -- I've gotten many of the books that taught me most via random browsing in such places, and I'd like other people to have the same fun.

3) No, I'm not that organized.

4) If I'm going to need to check it out repeatedly, and it's not too expensive, I buy it. On the other hand, a lot of books I need aren't available from libraries...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 2) It might happen that just before collapse copyrights become eternal. How would this affect the workings you did to protect your books?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I expect decline, not collapse, and no legal enactment is ever actually eternal. (In fact, when somebody passes a law that's supposed to last forever, you can bet your bottom dollar that the whole kit and caboodle is about to unravel.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My thoughts on the matter would be that as soon as the legal framework validating and enforcing the copyrights ceases to exist in any coherent fashion (i.e. the egregor dissipates), the ostensibly eternal copyright would go with it.

Otherwise, things like Roman law would, at least from an occult/karmic perspective, technically still be in force around the Meditteranean, since most laws aren't enacted with built-in sunset periods and I don't think the declining Roman Empire ever got around to actually repealing those laws... the establishment of new kingdoms and the supplanting of the Roman egregor with those kingdoms.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, it's still on the agenda.

2) Such investments will eventually become worthless -- how soon "eventually" is, of course, is the problem. I don't have any such investments myself because I don't plan on retiring -- I enjoy the work that I do, and expect to quit writing when they haul my cold dead fingers off the keyboard. I could be wrong, but if you're in your twenties the chance that you'll be able to retire at all seems very small to me.

3) It's not a typo. All Water signs are in the triplicity of Mars; Cancer is also the sign of Mars' fall, but the fact that it's also his triplicity makes it not quite as bad as it would otherwise be.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What would you say are the reasons for people on their twenties not being able to retire?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The idea of retirement -- the notion that after 30 years or so on the job you should be able to kick back and vacation for the rest of your life -- was only viable for a fairly brief historical interval, at the peak of the global economic book set in motion by the breakneck exploitation of fossil fuels, and even then it was only a minority of people in the rich countries of the world who ever really had that option. The Age of Extravagance is winding slowly down around us, and by the time people now in their twenties are 65 or so, there won't be the surplus wealth needed to support a large unproductive retiree population. If we're lucky and smart, there'll be arrangements made for the elderly who really can't work at all, but 20 years sitting on your rump in Florida? Not a chance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Probably the most prudent thing would be to hoard money for retirement or the Great Collapse/Lingering Collapse, whichever comes first.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, that's a good way to attract armed robbery or the like. If your preparations consist of a stockpile of stuff, you're simply going to come to the interest of people who want to separate you from your stash, and don't care whether you survive the process. If you want to do well in grubby times, learn skills that you can use to provide desirable goods and services for the people around you; that way everyone has a stake in your survival.

replies.title: hoarding money

If you really want to just save cash, you might want to diversify the types of "cash" you hold - perhaps some gold, some silver, I suppose there might be other options too: I am no expert, but I have read that 1 US dollar from a hundred years ago now has 5 cents buying power.

replies.title: Uh-oh, John, I have seen it... or how the magic has started to work
replies.content:
Alright, it is hardly magic at the stage I am at but just reading about it, doing meditations, divinations and journaling has brought great benefits to my life, even if just glimpses of the unseen. I think I even had my first experience with one of those mighty beings we call Gods. All in an all a sudden perspective which though frightening is so much worth it. The other day, it suddenly clicked while walking down the street and I “saw it”, not on paper on one of your writings but around me; I saw it on people’s faces, on what they were saying; I saw it on the ads... The Horror! It’s always been there, but now it was not on my skin and over my brains. I was at a distance from it, away from its influence. The terror of starring at it mixed with excitement was quite something.

I just noticed that it has been almost a year since I found out that Magic is not something scribbled on the crumbling pages of the past but a living thing that can be studied, practiced, experienced and further developed! As a kid I was excited by the idea of knowing and studying about the universe, a physics researcher that excelled at mathematics being my dream, going over Newton, Euclid, Kepler, Einstein, Grothendieck through the night. That didn’t work out well since I never actually got to doing it the way I wanted to for various reasons and my dreams were crushed. Who would’ve thought that I would see myself having that dream revived in a different, and much vivid and broader way, going through what much of the exact same scientists were also studying! I need a shirt that says: “Occultism has what you were looking for in Science.”

As a twenty something I find it troubling that also the concept of retirement is used along the lines of “if you give us your soul we’ll guarantee you safety later” or “doesn’t matter how much you hate your current situation you’ll enjoy retirement if you just keep doing it and someday, just someday you’ll do what you really want”. Basically, don’t live your life the way you want it, give it to us and you’ll be happy later! When? When you are too old, alone, your soul has been crushed and you posses no skills at all. What is the purpose of living and old age if you have never actually lived? Sounds like torture honestly. I mean, come on, it borders on the absurd. I saw an ad the other day that said “Retirement is when you have the time to learn how to surf” What! Surfing? The net maybe... if it lasts! Straight up manipulation. Infusing fear by the promise of youth. That is just... evil. It is very strange to see how, slowly, the people I started this job of mine with have dwindled and being beaten down one way or another on sucking up the lives and ideas that somebody else wants them to believe so that they can profit from it. And it almost happened to me! One day, soon enough, I’ll be out of this job doing something that I am passionate about because, in a big way, a Druid occultist writes about just how to do that tinted with laser focus commentaries on the absurdities of our Age of Extravagance. Thank you, it is a great source of strength!

Carnelian Crunchy Toad

comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

1. Can positive hedonism be compatible with occultism? Can a rational pleasure-seeker practice magic while avoiding the Ahrimanic pattern of evil? Can we imagine Keith Richards the archmage?

2. What, if any, are the karmic consequences of killing insects? Am I condemned to a bad reincarnation if I squash some spiders every once in a while, or put up some ant baits? I know that certain Buddhist sects advocate total pacifism, but I imagine most occultists don't go that far?

3. What are the karmic consequences of suicide due to depression? I know that this topic is very complex and distressing, but are there any generalities that can be drawn, since it is unfortunately a rather common occurrence in industrialized societies? What are the effects on the afterlife?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, of course. Occultism is not an ascetic path. If you practice your hedonism in an ethical manner -- that is to say, make sure that everyone involved in your carouses has a good time and nobody gets messed over -- it should be no hindrance at all; you'll just have to schedule your occult practices so that you get over the hangover first.

2) All life feeds on life, and all life struggles against life. Vines choke out trees, hawks tear out the throats of pigeons, and it's a rare elephant that doesn't flatten insects with every step. The advice I'd offer is don't kill when you don't have to; it requires only a little more effort to trap the spider with a plastic tumbler and a sheeet of card and toss it out the window, for example. If you have to, be as quick and as clean as you can.

3) If you kill yourself to run away from something in this life, you can guarantee that you'll have to deal with the same thing in your next life. Also, suicides generally have to hang around waiting until they would have died naturally before they can go on, and by all accounts that's very, very dull.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Basically, all our modern moralities are stuck in categories inherited from Christian moral thought, in which some things are right, some things are wrong, and the job of the ethicist is to tell other people what to do on the basis of some claim to moral absolutes. That makes perfect sense when you believe that moral rules are handed down by God, but if you abolish God it stops making any sense at all; you're left trying to insist that your personal value judgments aren't just personal value judgments, no, they're objective absolute truths! Since no two people make the same value judgments all the time, you're left with people shrieking at each other, because they have no basis for their claim that their value judgments are better than anyone else's.

Nietzsche's phrasing is as usual succinct: "Moral philosophy is the art of propping up inherited prejudices with bad logic."

comments.content:
Good evening Mr. Greer -

First, I want to thank you for taking the time to do this every Monday. Although I only rarely ask any questions, I read these threads almost every week and always walk away with something new to think about. After a years-long staring contest with the Watcher, however, I have finally resolved to take up serious study, rather than watching from the outside. I have begun working through the first volume of the Dolmen Arch, and am now about a week in. I am already intrigued and eager for more, although I’m making it a point to be patient and work through the material slowly and deliberately. I have a couple questions, though:

1) You have always been adamant that divination is an integral part of occult practice, and you have also noted previously that the Dolmen Arch system meshes with any system of divination the student cares to use. I don’t have any existing divinatory practice, and I have chosen to learn geomancy along with the material in the DA system – the big reason being your own characterization of geomancy as a straightforward-if-not-blunt oracle. Is there any reason that I should be wary of learning a divination system from scratch along with the DA system? You have mentioned before that it’s imperative to follow magical instruction to the letter and to not “mix and match” when learning a new system, so any advice you can give about whether this is prudent is appreciated.

2) Also with respect to divination, I have been casting my daily chart shortly after completing the EC+CL ritual and the first meditative exercise each day. I settled on a standard question: “what should I learn from today’s magical practice?” I have cast shield charts, and the past two days have given me the same outcome: LW Albus, RW Albus, J Populus. replies.title: Filtering and Cayce
replies.content:
Yes, one of Edgar Cayce’s more controversial readings concerned A. Hitler and the future of Germany in the mid-30’s. In this reading, Cayce seemed to dismiss the concerns of European Jews, in effect stating that though Hitler seemed to be antagonizing much of the world at that time, his policies were derived from deep meditation, that despite the world’s misunderstanding, his policies merited study and reflection. Though Cayce seemed to have no objection to Hitler’s status as a dictator, he had one caveat - all of Hitler’s designs for Germany would be brought to ruin if Hitler became imperialistic.

As it turns out, this reading was for a German-American individual who actually returned to Germany when the war broke out. One might surmise that Cayce’s reading was “filtered” considerably by the inclinations of the person who requested it.

In another controversial reading, Cayce indicated that though black Americans were surely human beings, they had not yet evolved to the point of achieving full citizenship - something to that effect. The reading was for Cayce’s father, an old Confederate and a guy who had about the same regard for Afro-Ams as did his fellow Virginian Woodrow Wilson. Again, one could surmise the reading to have been considerably filtered by the reading requester.

My impression is that Cayce was at his best re health and holistic medicines. When he strayed into cultural/political territory, he was often easily influenced in his readings by the attitudes and beliefs of those who had requested the readings.

Will M

When one contradicts the other, which side does occult philosophy come down on?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The thing that incensed Plotinus most about the Gnostics he encountered is that they thought that matter and the material world were evil, and that their creator was an evil being. From a Neoplatonist perspective, that's the worst sort of blasphemy! The nondualist Gnostic sects tended to be much more compatible with Neoplatonism. As for occult philosophy, the majority of occult philosophical traditions come down hard on the Neoplatonist/nondualist side, but there are exceptions.

comments.title: Books and Geomancy
comments.content:
If one wanted to, this could probably be sorted into the categories "over-doing" or "over-thinking" it - why not just pick up a book and read it and then the next? Still, since I have not yet had the pleasure to consult geomancy on book issues, I welcomed the opportunity to practice and tried to find out which out of four books would be the most sensible to start with. So I did a reading for each asking the question "Would starting with book X benefit me most?" - 5th house question, obviously.

For two books the chart did not perfect. For two it did and I wonder which reading "scores higher", so to say.

Book 1: 1-amissio, 2-rubeus, 3-puer, 4-populus, 5-amissio, 6-conjunctio, 7-laetitia, 8-albus, 9-cauda draconis, 10-puer, 11-fortuna minor, 12-amissio, rw-fortuna major, lw-conjunctio, rw-acquisitio
Book 2: 1-fortuna minor, 2-fortuna major, 3-laetitia, 4-fortuna minor, 5-puella, 6-carcer, 7-rubeus, 8-rubeus, 9-via, 10-rubeus, 11-albus, 12-populus, rw-puella, lw-albus, j-carcer

Reading one perfects by occupation and conjunction, but does this with amissio, which one could interpret negatively as a "yes, but...". Still, considering the phrasing of the question I wonder whether this has to be read as a "yes, because...". After all, amissio stand for loosing something and passes to the house of limitations. The main reason I started this little reading project for is exploring the reasons for fear and limitations and possibly overcoming it, so that would make sense. Additionally the court is quite positive, which maybe does not count too much in house readings, but still... Additionally, the cardines are not too bad for this one, I'd say.

Reading two perfects by conjunction "only" and suggest to me, that with this book I could score a "quick win", but possibly would not get too much out of it. The court is not very positive and with carcer being the judge underscores the theme of not getting too much out of it. The cardines are not very positive, either. However, the figures in 1 and 5 are comparably positive ones compared to amissio for book 1.

So basically I wonder which interpretation of the first reading makes more sense since that directly ranks reading two higher or lower:

1) "Book 1 is exactly where you have to go to. You want to find out something to reduce limitations and fear, there you will find what you're looking for" or
2) "Book 1 is very important but at that moment a loss/waste of time"

A second question on reading two: Rubeus shows up fairly often in the chart but without a direct connection to the relevant figures. In your experience, can you treat the figures just as building blocks that have to be there so that the rest of the chart can look as it has to, or has such a constellation any significance beyond this?

After all I'm probably going to read both books and find out - but still, since this is a little geomancy exercise, I'd be really interested in your opinion on how to compare those two readings.

Thanks & greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.content: Yes, I think it counts as overthinking it! I'd take the readings as saying that you can read either book #1 or book #2 first, with a slight advantage to book #1, simply because it has two modes of perfection rather than one.

replies.title: trepanning
replies.content:
Years ago on a late night radio program I heard an interview with a man who claimed that a hole in the skull increased the circulation by decreasing pressure. He said that some people achieved this accidentally by a fall during childhood, or some other accident that prevented complete closure or the sutures in the skull. He claimed that one sign that this had occurred was clear memories of what it was like to have been a child. He had actually drilled a hole in his own forehead with a brace and bit (yeah, that made me go whoa). Now the thing that made me remember and think about this as something more that a weird crackpot notion is that I do have clear memories of how I felt about things as a child while observing that many adults I encounter seem to have forgotten. I mean could any well intended parent throw out a child's worn out teddy if they remembered how much they had loved their favorite toy? And other dismissals of childish emotions that just make me thing 'dude, were you ever a kid or did you just hatch fully grown like some kind of insect?'. I don't actually believe this guy's theory (wonder what happened to him) but I do know that there is an active trepanning community out there.

OTH idealizing childhood can be seen as just another way of idealizing the primitive.

Rita

comments.title: The Fool
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Hope all is well with you and yours.

For a long time now I've been incredibly interested in the court fool and more recently Harlequins and the link with my particular choice of martial arts.

My schools head is in his late 80s and is the mirror opposite of what is considered 'normal' in Japan for marital arts. He trains in sweatpants, has pink hair, has tea break where he'll paint anything for anyone, and we are all able to attend his training sessions regardless of rank. Everything about training is non-standard to Japanese and Western eyes. Rank is essentially worthless, and frequently used a rope for folks to hang themselves.... or an quiet reminder your doing ok,.... or both?!? He has an exquisite way of giving everything to everyone; you want to be the big Daishihan? no problem- rank, medals etc are yours!, you want to sit in the corner and train? He'll pop over and say exactly the right thing that will invariable cause an 'aha!! moment (although not always at that point in time, sometimes later on a packed train were you start laughing to yourself, not helping the strange foreigner stereotype :P)

Training has this carnival feel to it, but when you really start to think about it- and frequently what fits in the spaces not show- it's terrifying (a feeling you get when he demos a technique on you, not what is done -which is bad enough- but what could be done, and how the folly fades to something quite leathal) all done with a huge smile and a laugh. There are also times were he purposefully teaches complete nonsense, or has extended tea-breaks if the class makeup has undesirables, or one of the alphabet soup US gov representatives based in Tokyo has shown up with their followers.

Can you think of a western school of thought, or thinker that follow this line of teaching? Folly mixed with the deadly serious? Blatantly poking fun at the overtly stiff and serious nature of something(or more perhaps how it can manifest if not done in a balanced way), while simultaneously dedicating yourself wholeheartedly to the study of it?

I keep coming back to the fool/jester and more specifically harlequins as a point of reference. Do you have any reading references about their training, or how they were able to move so seamlessly though societal strata, offer deep insight into it, mock it, and not loose their heads? Are they specifically associated with a specific mystery school? One of the themes that is frequently discussed in class is 虚実 (kyojitsu), or the interplay of truth and falsehood, which again links me back to the fool or harlequin. Bright gaudy colors, while hidden behind a mask; gymnastics and tomfoolery, which I'm sure could be quite quickly made lethal as the movements are interrelated; silly rhymes with underlying depth. Any insights appricated.

p.s while chatting to my wife trying to think of a way to write this, I made her think of this book: The Music of the Spheres: Music, Science and the Natural Order of the Universe. New York: Springer Verlag, 1995. Seems right up your street! Interesting too, how so many things can be interrelated: martial arts, mystery schools, and music! cheers :)

A traditional kata then gets henka applied aka, endless 'what ifs' or variation. What if the opponent strikes like a boxer or what if in the first instance I move inside, rather than out, what if he's armed with a stick, and I'm not, a sword and I'm not etc etc ad infinitum. We are constantly encouraged to go and play with the technique and what you notice in the Japanese Shihan is the art manifests itself in ways that as an outsider you wouldn't consider their movement to be related, it gives them the freedom to move in ways that work for their particular body type and personalities: one teacher is a dancer by trade, the other a former champion kick-boxer, another essentially a vagabond who traveled all over the place with most of his possession on his back, all manifest their technique in different ways; but at it's core its the same taijutsu, or body movement.

All that said, this essentially complete freedom comes with a great responsibility to keep yourself in check. Can you teach the fundamentals correctly? as if you go off piste and fail your on your own. As are your students. Are you training with a benevolent heart? If not and you are learning to hurt others, you'll frequently end up owning yourself. Something you see with the teachers who are still around, and are allowed to be around, if not encouraged, to continue to fail with the idea that a) it's up to us to find a good teacher and b) like attracts like.

Church of the Sub-Genius, and the rowdier end of chaos magic still a good match? thx

comments.title: Freeze
comments.content:
Dear JMG, a data point for you, from the hills east of Cumberland. We had a hard freeze here on the farm on Saturday morning. A freeze in May is extremely rare; the last one I can remember was over 20 years ago. I can now say that we have lost 100% of our cherries, peaches, strawberries, and blueberries. There is a lot of damage to other crops but I am hoping we get at least some apples and grapes. Some large non-fruit trees have turned fully black. Other trees seem to be doing better, depending on the species.


The freeze was especially damaging (and unusual) because it happened with high winds. I'll explain this more fully. Orchards in the Appalachians are often planted up on the hillsides. Typically a late frost or freeze will happen on a calm clear night where outgoing longwave radiation is maximized. The ground surface gets very cold, and the air near the ground gets very cold. Since cold air sinks, it drains down the hillsides into the valleys. So with a typical late spring freeze scenario, the valleys get a hard freeze, but the trees on the hillsides only freeze for a few feet above the ground, which is not usually damaging.

On Saturday morning, the freeze came with high winds, so there was no protection on the hillsides. On Sunday morning, we had another freeze, this time a more typical freeze, where the valleys were hit the hardest. So no one escaped. Yet another frost/freeze is forecast for tonight. At this point the damage is done, I think. Even the spring emerald green lawn is turning brown.

I only know what happened to us on our farm. But I believe the damage may be widespread through large portions of the east. I expect there's going to be a lot of hurt for many regions that are already hurting.

I know a fair amount about weather and climate, and I can say the weather pattern for this freeze event was and is something like I've not seen in my 50 years of following the weather on a daily basis. One might wonder if the abrupt reduction in global air pollution is having any role in this.

Anyway, here's my question for you (which I am having trouble finding the right words for). What could you say about the magical aspects of this sort of weather event / natural disaster? I have been mulling over the process of change in the US and the role of the "changer" archetype. Natural disasters can certainly reinforce change that is already underway. Do you see the possibility of forces at work that would use natural disasters as a tool to nudge things in the "changer" direction?

replies.title: Re: Freeze
replies.content: Wow -- that's got to be a challenge. I hope the trees recover next year. Yes, this could be being driven by magical energies seeking to force change; it could also simply be the consequences of an increasingly unstable climate due to all the crap we're dumping into the atmosphere out of our tailpipes and smokestacks.

comments.title: A dream
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am really glad about this forum. It is interesting to read what is on other people’s minds, and I can confirm I often find things that I have been thinking about myself. Also, reading about other people’s progress and experience with the practices is very encouraging and helpful (thanks, Maxine...).

I dropped my original questions that I had written down during the week, because recent events diverted my attention to something else. I had another dream about you, JMG. You may remember that the first dream made me join this forum. This time it was again one of those that make me think for a long time. The evening before the dream I had an unusual ogham reading. I struggled with the question at first because of what had been said here on MM about the importance of precise wording. No question seemed precise enough to me until finally I asked: What should I ask you? The reading was interesting, but not completely clear to me and I remember a thought flicker somewhere at the back of my mind that a dream would come in handy for dealing with it. I did not focus on it, but it came anyway.
The dream went like this: I was sitting at a bar on a high stool. You were sitting next to me being invisible. I told you you could show up because I could sense you anyway. I needed to talk to you. So we talked, but I don’t know what about. Then your wife appeared from somewhere inside the bar and was being very protective of you. She didn’t want me to talk to you. In the end you and your wife decided to go back home to the mountains where you lived. I accompanied you to the mountainside. At one point you separated from us and went to a hole or a small cave in the side of the mountain. You were carrying a thick book. I couldn’t see it, but I had a feeling the book was something between a journal and a photo album. I was watching you from afar as you put it into the cave and left it there. Then you came back to me to say goodbye. I had tears in my eyes, but the feeling is very difficult to describe. A little bit of everything. Then I woke up.

I had an idea what the dream meant but I consulted ogham to give me its point of view. One of the symbols of the previous evening repeated. Ioho reversed. I associate Ioho with endings, a portal into the darkness and the unknown. It also spoke of attraction and possibilities. This is what I make of the dream: You represented magic itself. I have felt drawn to it for a long time and you are the person who kindled this desire in me. Your wife was the equivalent of the Watcher. (Say hello to her, please. I will be relieved if she finds it at least mildly amusing ;-) ) I originally thought the book was yours until I realized it might have been mine. My life up until now.

I should probably put it in a broader context. I first started practising meditation and ritual in autumn 2012. I went on for several months and I consider this practice one of the main reasons that made me leave my job. From there an improbable series of events unfolded. I was offered a new job without looking for it, on a personal level I went through the worst time of my life when I felt as if my whole personality disintegrated and I had to build my identity again from scratch. I was being destructive.The process was very painful, and not only for me. At some point I felt as if I was going through a stage of a pupa and I held onto the hope that I would be reborn as something more mature and stable. In the end I did. It was over, and probably for the first time in my life I experienced a feeling of deep inner peace. I was also filled with an enormous sense of gratitude because I knew somebody was watching over me and led me through all this. I still needed some time to recover, but last autumn, seven years after my first attempt, I resumed my full practice and left my job again. I hadn’t done anything during the seven years apart from intensive journaling, writing poems and occasional divination.

I am sorry for the length of this, but this was the shortest I could make it. My dilemma now is this: I feel in an important stage of my practice. About two weeks ago I had several days in a row when I felt an unprecedented repulsion towards practices and I stopped for a few days. It felt strange, ogham spoke of a trial and challenge. I resumed with heightened effort. The dream followed. Since then I have been questioning my motivation in magical practice. I take it seriously, it is not just a game for me, although I know it can be playful and I suspect ogham sometimes even shows sense of humour. I certainly laughed with it more than once. Anyway, I have always felt I have been blessed with many gifts in my life. After overcoming the difficult period even more so. I feel I owe it to the world to give something back. I tried to do things before, joined environmental groups, or even initiated one myself, it just didn’t feel like me. I wasn’t suited very well for this kind of interactions. Now I feel I could perhaps be a better asset to the world through the practice of magic. Unlike other things, it doesn’t come easily to me. It is a real challenge and I only have a very vague idea of where the path leads. I am not sure I have what it takes to make a sufficient progress or even whether I am worthy of it. I already know I can help myself through magic, but I am not sure it is enough. I just feel I may need to have an answer ready when I face doubt or discouragement. Do you think I should have any other reason at hand apart from: I trust my intuition that this is my way?

I will be thankful for your insight. Next time I will keep my post short, I promise.

Hana

replies.title: Re: A dream
replies.content: This is a common stage in the Path.  The question of whether you have what it takes to be a mage, or whether you're worthy of it, will be settled by your own decision in the time immediately ahead of you.  If you shove aside all your doubts and worries, and just keep on practicing, then you have what it takes and you're worthy of the Path. If not, well, you can do the math yourself. This is the test that everybody faces when they get serious about magic: will you keep following the Path, or not?  And of course you and only you can make that decision.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How well do theories of morphic fields and the hundredth monkey effect match up with occult philosophy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, those are two very different things.

The theory of morphic fields was developed by Dr. Rupert Sheldrake on the basis of extensive experimentation and introduced in his book A New Science of Life in 1981. It corresponds very closely to occult philosophy -- enough so that I'm pretty sure Sheldrake himself is familiar with Dion Fortune's The Cosmic Doctrine, which includes a very similar take on the universe.

The "hundredth monkey effect," by contrast, is a fraud. Lyall Watson made it up for his 1979 book Lifetide; if you dig up the original papers by the scientists who ran the study -- yes, I have -- you'll find that Lyall lied about what happened. (The monkeys learned how to wash sand off sweet potatoes in the usual way, starting with a few clever young monkeys and gradually spreading through the group; a decade later there were still grumpy old monkeys who weren't washing their sweet potatoes.) There used to be a fine article with the winsome title "Debunking the Spud-Dunking Monkeys," but it seems to have dropped off the internet.

The "hundredth monkey effect" also very clearly doesn't work in practice. We've certainly had critical masses of people who believed in Marxism, in Fascism, in racial superiority, in various dogmatic religions, etc., etc. -- you'll notice that everyone didn't suddenly start agreeing with them...

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content:
1) What an excellent theme for your next week or two of meditation! I mean this quite seriously -- if you've got enough background to ask that question, you've got enough background to work through it in meditation and develop your capacity to approach such things on the mental plane.

comments.title: Rationalist Applecart Requires Inversion
comments.content:
I've been doing the CGD Bardic pathworkings, and I've noticed that most of my subsequent meditations revolve around "what part of my life experience prior to the pathworking would lead to my brain throwing X into it?" On a particularly insightful day, I might get so far as "If I am seeing X in path Y, what does that tell me about myself that I might not have noticed?" But in general, I mostly focus on the personal psychological aspect of it.

This is fine, but there are symbolic elements to the work such as the elemental correspondences to the groves and the geomancy symbols for the paths and groves. Though I've been meditating over the relevant symbols and try to go looking for them in each pathworking, I'm having trouble figuring out what to do with them when I find them. Usually they get tied back to something like "well I saw X in the grove of earth so it probably has something to do with my material circumstances."

I'm assuming since I'm getting some insights from pursuing the psychological side of the pathworkings that its no trouble to keep noticing them. My question is what dimensions of pathworking interpretation am I missing here, and what additional questions should I be asking in my meditations to expand my understanding?

replies.title: Re: Rationalist Applecart Requires Inversion
replies.content: Relax. Most students focus on one or another aspect of the pathworking experience their first time through, and that's fine. A few years from now you'll likely decide to go back through the paths again, and pick up some of the other things you can get from them. (I've been through those particular pathworkings three times now, with different results each time, and there'll doubtless be a fourth pass waiting for me in a few years.)

comments.title: The Fermi Paradox and Alien Gods
comments.content:
Hell Mr. Greer,

Maybe I've been looking at too much H.P. Lovecraft lately but there is a particular question I have about aliens/gods. You have noted many times that the possible reason why we have never made any contact with little green men and other science fiction equivalents of extra terrestrial life is that the vastness of space does not allow for realistic travel given our energy limitations. Possible fuels might build skyscrapers but they can't realistically create interplanetary travel. My question is, does this restriction also apply to gods, spirits, angels, and so on? Once we stop talking about carbon based lifeforms and start talking about things on the astral plane (or any of the non material planes for that matter) does the Fermi Paradox still apply?

replies.title: Re: The Fermi Paradox and Alien Gods
replies.content: Once you're beyond the material world the Fermi paradox as such no longer applies, because the basis for it -- the fact that we gaze out into the heavens and see no evidence of advanced interstellar civilizations -- no longer applies. Gaze out into the heavens on some of the other planes and you'll see quite a bit! Those occult writers who've really grasped the implications of the scale of the cosmos -- Dion Fortune among them -- have said that it appears as though each solar system is more or less a self-contained unit, with its own Solar Logos, Star Logoi, planetary spirits, and other divine and quasi-divine beings, but that doesn't foreclose the possibility of exceptions.

comments.title: 'Fictional' deities
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've seen you mention here a couple of times about 'fictional results from fictional deities' but here's my question - how can you tell if a deity really is fictional, or if it has just been contacted by the creator of some literature, or brought into existence by the collective action of many minds considering it?

I ask this, as recently a name has kept appearing to me in my mind of 'The lord of rage'. I didn't really think much of this for a few months, but decided to look it up the other week and found that, as well as being the name of a thrash metal band, in the Warhammer 40 000 universe, the Chaos god Khorne's name is supposed to translate directly to this phrase, which I found mildly alarming.

I was dimly familiar with the lore of Warhammer 40 000 (I had a few friends who played it some years ago, so read up on it a little, but never read any of the books or played the tabletop games myself), so I suppose there is a chance I just remembered it, but I also know that Warhammer lore was influenced by Lovecraft, which you said may reflect the reality (whatever that might be :D ) of the Great Old Ones. There have obviously also been a lot of games of Warhammer played and a lot of books and other literature made about it over the years, so the Warhammer universe, and by extension, its deities, may have their own egregores and 'tracks in space' by now.

I took up daily banishing, meditation and prayer from around December, and have noticed that I do seem to be less angry/irritable since.

So my questions are these:
- Is it likely there actually is a god behind this which has, for whatever reason, taken an interest in me and goes by aforementioned name? Do rage gods (rather than just gods with a bad attitude) exist in any pantheons that you know of, and could one have arisen from the Warhammer egregore? Or is it more likely to be a malevolent imp or similar just impersonating such a god? Are the steps for politely declining their presence the same in both cases?

- What else, other than banishing, meditation and prayer, and practices such as cold showers and breathing exercises (all of which I've been doing) can dispel anger, as I'm keen to loosen any grip such entities might have on me? I've been trying to minimise my exposure to 'triggers' or 'primers' which I found usually tend to be related to computers in some way (eg. video games, and some music, such as electronic dance music) but when I'm currently working from home all day on the laptop (with the dreaded Windows 10!) that's quite hard to do...

As an aside, I was also *really* against getting a dog when my wife wanted to last year, and for the first month or so after we got her, it was like something in me just wanted the dog gone. I know you mentioned last week how dogs can sense ghosts and spirits - can they also repel demons? Our house, which I've never really felt comfortable in since we moved in 2 years ago, seems a little more 'at rest' after we got her, but that could also be the banishing, which curiously enough, I started at around the same time where I started to accept her in my life more... (we both love her now though)

Sorry for the long post!

replies.title: Re: 'Fictional' deities
replies.content:
Interesting. A powerful egregor can affect the human mind in roughly the same way as a minor-to-middling deity; the main difference is that the egregor isn't intelligent, and simply repeats its influence in a broken-record way. I don't have any way of telling whether Khorne is simply a well-charged egregor, or whether some entity or other has taken over the shell of energies and is using that for its own purposes -- yes, this also happens.

Prayer, ritual, and meditation are the standard tools for this sort of thing; also reflection and self-control, but you're already doing that. I'm not aware of any traditional lore that says that dogs can chase out spooks, but who knows?

replies.title: Re: 'Fictional' deities
replies.content: AVOID IT! Seriously, if you know anything about Warhammer 40K lore, you know how nasty it is, and I had to drop the game once it became way too clear to me there was something on the other end....

replies.title: if I may ask...?
replies.content:
You do not wish to pay respects to the Queen of Heaven and Fount of Motherhood? Not even once a year on Mother’s Day or during a Full Scorpio Moon on behalf of all women who go into dangerous labor and especially in places where maternal mortality is high due to poverty and malnutrition and other material causes? You would not wish to invoke her aid on their behalf? Nor acknowledge her protection of the home? I do not understand your choice. Surely she deserves some portion of honor and respect for her roles in blessing the emergence of new life, enduring mother love, and bestowing the gifts of material prosperity.

The story of Hippolytus who disdained Aphrodite as kakistein daimonon (worst of the gods) comes to mind. Though she did not make him bow to her power directly by making him fall in love, she made the love of others bring about his downfall. His stepmother being attracted to him sexually; fear of shame and exposure driving her to false accusations after her old nurse -- out of love for her darling nursling – exposing what had better been kept hidden; the jealous love of his own father for his new young wife—powerful stuff. What Hera could bring to bear on those who dishonor her is significant to the same degree. Something to think about, anyway.

replies.title: Re: Kek and the collapse of the Democrats

Yes and yes.

1) The Democrats are unusually vulnerable to this sort of thing because of the widening gap between beliefs and actions that has been so massive a feature of the party in recent decades. Think about the way they glorified Obama for exactly the same drone strikes they denounced savagely when Bush II did them, or for that matter the way #BelieveAllWomen has become #BelieveJoeBiden. That kind of hypocrisy has many downsides, but the worst is that it makes reflective thinking impossible, and someone who is unable or unwilling to reflect on their thoughts and actions is a sitting duck for hostile magic of the sort that the mages of Kekistan are ready, willing, and able to unleash.

2) That said, "unusually vulnerable" does not mean that others aren't potentially at risk from the same sort of magic. Nasty magic is very common in today's world; when an advertisement makes you think that buying some product will make you happy and sexy and popular with your preferred gender, that's the same kind of sorcery the alt-right chaos mages are using -- it has a different target, but it's just as precisely aimed at making you behave in ways that run counter to your own interests. This is why basic spiritual hygiene is so important. As a Christian in one of the traditional churches you've got plenty of resources to draw on for that; since so many people aren't involved in such traditions and have valid reasons not to be, I recommend methods drawn from the occult tradition for their use.

We live in a time when the spiritual and magical energies surrounding us are very murky, tangled, and difficult. Hostile magic is a constant reality, and needs to be taken seriously -- and the Democrats are a great example of what happens when you don't do so.

comments.content: Second question: can anyone here recommend a Tarot deck for someone with freakishly small hands and dubious coordination? Mine keep getting quite battered, in part because the cards are too large for me to manage shuffling without a fair amount of brute force.

replies.title: small tarot decks
replies.content:
The way I cope with this is not to shuffle them like playing cards. I cut the cards, hold one half in my weak hand, long edge up, cut the cards, insert various cards from the cut in between the other half at random intervals, repeat several times. Sometimes I pull out a few cards, reverse them, and stick them back in. It's slow and clumsy, but it's the way I learned to do it.

Back in the Sixties, I recollect the BOTA (Brothers of the Adytum) deck as being quite small. It was printed in black and white on uncoated card stock, and the box contained instructions for coloring the trumps. The number cards had no pictures, just suit symbols like pre-19th century decks. The illustrations on the trumps are very similar to those on the Waite-Smith deck. You can order these by mail.

Another small B&W deck which is unfortunately out of print is the The New Tarot by Hurley and Horler. I like this deck, but it has nonstandard attributions of the elements to the suits. You might find it on EBay.

You can order a miniature version of the Waite-Smith Tarot, but it is really small. If your baby doll was a Tarot reader, this deck would be the perfect size for her.

There probably are others, but I don't know about them.

If you want to do cartomancy but are not attached to Tarot, traditional Lenormand decks and the Gypsy Witch deck which is an offshoot of Lenormand are the size of regular playing cards or smaller. These days, some of the artier Lenormand decks are large, so pay attention to the description on the website if ordering online.

Umber Somnolent Skull

comments.title: Philosophical divide
comments.content:
I am a heathen. Lately I have seen several views within the heathen sphere clash. I don't mean political views here, but they often overlap with politics.

There are animists, who see heathenry(and modern paganism in general) primarily as being local, tribe or family based, and who prioritize ties to ancestors, land, and spirits as primary. They tend to have an animistic view of the gods and spirits, seeing the gods as more powerful spirits. These tend not to value a complicated hierarchy, and many are environmentalists. There is little concern with a metaphysic.

Then there are others, who seem to be influenced by forms of occultism, Neoplatonism, Vedanta, and people like Rene Guenon and Julius Evola. Many of them see animism as some kind of "primitive regression" and not "Tradition" in the strict sense. They tend to see connection to a sacral hierarchy as more important than mere tribe. The gods are seen as forces that are contacted through rituals, and I have seen talk that has the gods as "expressions" of some ultimate principle that is indescribable and unprovable. A common end goal is basically like Buddhism, and there is talk of the "Kali Yuga". I know that Evola did not consider the gods to be "real" in a personal sense, but as higher forces that required the mediation of ritual to make use of.

I recently talked with someone about shamanism that held the latter view, very much influenced by Evola and Guenon. According to them, shamanism only contacts lower beings, and is a degeneration because it is not in contact with some supreme principle(which, I was told, is non-being). I am not even certain how one can be in contact with non-being.

I have some regard for Greek philosophy, but I am still wary of much of it. Platonism in particular tries to rationalize the world in a way that I don't think is really possible. The philosophers always want a model world that conforms to their desires, as I see it. They take their own fancies and label them "The Good" and multiply all sorts of first principles, levels, and abstractions. I don't see much in Traditionalism but a mixture of neoplatonic perennialism and Theosophy. It would be heresy in all the major religions, including Islam.

My question is this: Do you think there was some root tradition that everything else came from? It is supposed to be a kind of complete metaphysic. Why do these philosophies seem to arise in periods of decline in older religious rites? Why do these metaphysicians often find themselves at odds with traditional religion? Plato, Socrates, Anaxagoras, Shankara, Gautama, Ibn Arabi, al Hallaj, Porphyry, the list is a long one. I don't interpret everything in myths literally, but I don't see in them a lot of concern with "escaping reincarnation by annihilation into the supreme principle" or "escaping the material realm". That sounds more like Sufism or Buddhism.

replies.title: Re: Philosophical divide
replies.content:
Regarding Rene Guenon,
Rene Guenon was a very revered teacher amongst some Sufis, some others in his group were Martin Lings and Charles Le Gai Eaton. In later life Guenon was involved in inappropriate sexual behavior with a young lady who participated in one of his rituals and was discredited.
People in the order hypothesized that something went wrong with him after he engaged in a long period of seclusion.

replies.title: Re: Philosophical divide
replies.content:
From my perspective, the Traditionalists are smoking their shorts. Neither Evola nor Guenon spoke for any authentic tradition; quite the contrary, they each patched together a grab-bag of influences from a mix of fashionable foreign spiritualities and the pop culture of their time, proclaimed it the One True Tradition, and used it as a club to beat other people over the head with. They were basically engaged in the same sort of cherrypicking activity that gave Aldous Huxley his "Perennial Philosophy," and also underlay such projects as the Theosophical Society.

There is no one root tradition that everything else comes from. There are traditions, plural, that emerge from human interaction with the spiritual realm; these traditions are constantly growing, changing, cross-pollinating, and contending with each other, like everything else in the world of manifestation. None of them are uniquely right; all of them have valuable things to teach -- and when somebody goes around insisting that your spirituality is inferior because it isn't theirs, you can take it for granted that what's going on has much more to do with an overinflated ego than with anything authentically spiritual.

Does that mean that Traditionalism is bad? Of course not. A mix of Neoplatonic perennialism and Theosophy seems to meet some people's spiritual needs very well. I just wish they'd get over themselves a bit and stop channeling their inner Biggus Dickus quite so much!

replies.title: Re: Philosophical divide
replies.content: How about shamanism? It doesn't have a philosophy but there are some patterns that repeat in almost every version of shamanism around the world.

replies.title: Re: Philosophical divide
replies.content: The common elements in quasi-shamanic systems have been grossly exaggerated by anthropologists looking for shamans under every rock and tree. There's certainly a specific, very ancient tradition that has its roots in northern Siberia, extending west as far as Scandinavia (where it influenced the mythology of Odin) and east across the Bering Strait to the Americas (which were populated from, ahem, Siberia); that's a specific thing, with lots of local and regional variations, and its different forms have common features for the same reason that all the different forms of Christianity have common features -- because they come from a common source. Outside of that? A lot of vague generalizations and dubious logic covering up dramatic differences.

comments.title: Steppenwolf
comments.content:
Many thanks for Magic Monday! Recently I've been thinking a lot about Herman Hesse's _Steppenwolf_, which to my mind is the amazingly honest and touching story of an individuation crisis in which the character engages with the autonomous contents of his own psyche and ultimately finds healing. Indeed, much more than the pessimism or the drug use, I find the healing in the book the most incredibly profound portion, profound in the sense that it provokes in me a near-classical catharsis.

My question is why do you think folks in the 1960's counterculture so violently misunderstood this book...and yet still engaged with it at all? I find this baffling: it's one thing to ignore something and another thing to get it, but quite another to engage with something with profound misunderstanding. This combination strikes me as a very profound riddle, and so I ask what your insights on it may be?

I have never been able to make sense of the weird way that so many people in the 1960s read it obsessively and just as obsessively misunderstood it. I wonder sometimes if they wished they had the courage to enter the Magic Theater, but never quite could do it...

replies.content:
3) You've already hit on the most important thing, which is simply to put in the time and effort, on a daily basis. I would also make sure that whatever work you're doing involves translating into the target language; that is, figuring out how to say things in Greek, or Latin, or whatever it is.

Of course, if you are able to spend time with a teacher or more advanced student, speak the "dead" language anyway! Even listening to recordings intended for students could be useful here.

You don't mention what language it is you're trying to learn, but if it's classical Greek (as I might guess from your reference to Plato), I had a good experience doing self-study with Donald Mastronarde's Introduction to Attic Greek, which has the great advantage of an answer key to all the exercises (including the ones that have you writing in Greek). The answer key is a separate paperback volume from the main text, but was easily available and inexpensive back when I learned. I'm not sure what else might be available these days.

For other classical languages (Latin and Sanskrit) I had the advantage of an in-person teacher who could check my work, so there was a much wider range of effective materials.

replies.title: languages
replies.content:
If I may give an answer for #3, I am a language teacher and aspiring polyglot. The key to learning a language is meaningful input.By that I mean reading or listening to something you understand, where the meaning is transparent and the content is engaging. I wouldn't recommend flashcards at all, because although the meaning is transparent (just flip the card) the content isn't usually engaging. One of the reasons conversation is recommended is because often people like chitchat- it is engaging, and you can talk about things you are interested in.
When I have played with Latin, I learned botanical terms, because I am interested in plants and gardening. If I had gone further with it, I would have found adaptations of Cato's De Agricultura and read(and re-read) that. Again, I like plants, so that content would be engaging. In the beginning especially, find content that you already know something about, and that you think is interesting, and that will help motivate you to keep going. Since you are reading Plato already, find an adaptation, perhaps a side by side version, and read them together.
-Katsmama

comments.title: guided meditation
comments.content:
A group I belong to has decided to do guided meditation more often than we did in the past. We have a stock of existing written out meditations, which we sometimes modify.

There is a very wide range within our group as to how people respond to being led through these meditations. At one extreme, some are right there, experiencing the journey vividly, seeing or otherwise being aware of all the other people in the group, interacting with any entity we go to meet, receiving messages or insights beyond what is suggested in the script, having profound experiences. At the other end, we have people struggling not to fall asleep, or hearing the words of the leader but not experiencing anything much.

The meditations are useful to the group's work. The meditation leaders are competent. Do you have an idea of why some people find it hard to get into the mental state needed to participate in guided meditations? Is it just an innate difference in psychology, suggestibility, psychic talent? Are there things people can do to improve their ability to get into the right state, if that's the issue, or to sense what is going on, if that's the issue?

Would simpler or more repetitious meditations help the people who can't get into them? If so, can this be done without boring the people who are proficient?

replies.title: Re: guided meditation
replies.content: As far as I know it's an inherent difference in mental structure, and there's nothing that can be done about it. See if you can find some other exercise to do along with the guided meditations, at which those less proficient at guided meditation happen to excel.

replies.title: Re: guided meditation
replies.content:
Thank you. I'm sorry to hear that, as I am one of the people who has difficulty getting anything out of the practice, and I was hoping to find a way to participate more fully. After the first couple of minutes, I get bored because the scenes aren't real to me and eventually my mind wanders back where my body is.

It's not that I am unable to visualize, or to imagine scenes where something is going on. I can do simple quick visualizations like the ones in the Sphere of Protection, and I have daydreams.

Pathworking is a central practice in some of the magical systems coming out of Britain, and seems to be very useful.

If others who have done guided meditation or pathworking in a group have comments or suggestions, feel free to share them.

comments.title: Developing the Mental Sheath
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer,

Can the study of mathematics assist in developing the Mental Sheath, & if so, what are the most effective means or aspects of cognition within it to do so? replies.title: Re: Developing the Mental Sheath
replies.content: Dion Fortune recommended mathematics for the development of the mind; so, of course, did Pythagoras. That being the case, yes, very likely. replies.title: Re: Developing the Mental Sheath
replies.content:
Don't rule out practical mathematics, either, joshuarout !

I bought one of those Moleskine books a number of years ago, and turned it into a book of geometry constructions: how to find the mid-angle halfway through an angle; how to construct a dodecagram on a given diagonal of a pentagon; how to construct a golden rectangle; and so on.

That, plus learning to use a slide rule and an abacus (and a class or two in basic bookkeeping and accounting) has been enormously valuable to me in terms of developing the mental sheath. There's a reason that Arithmetic and Geometry are part of the quadrivium!

comments.title: Bio Dreams
comments.content:
Infrequently but regularly, during this calendar year, I've been having "bio dreams." A typical one begins with my discovering something amiss, usually unexpected water, in some location (which is not in my real-world house). It might be a film of water tricking down a wall, or an inch of standing water on an expansive tile floor. Looking closer I find discolorations, things growing in the moisture, resembling algae, mosses, or molds. Which then turn out to harbor tiny mites or worms. I find this alarming and repulsive at first, but that gradually gives way to fascination as colonies or swarms of larger and more complex organisms appear (that is, come to my attention, as if they'd been there all along but I'm only noticing them by turn), fungoids, polyp forms, coelenterates, and other even more intricate forms, the largest being fish-like (if underwater) or crustacean-like. Some are quite colorful and beautiful, but they're never recognizable to me from any era of natural history I'm familiar with. (Except, well, worms always look pretty much the same.) There's no further "plot" to the dreams, only ending with either waking or passing into some other less memorable dream.

I have reason to believe these dreams are a calling card (as in, a nod across the room, not an invitation to tea and scones) of one or more of the nature deities or higher spirits I've been asking my local nature spirits to pass my regards along to. (For one thing, although I might conceivably be capable of inventing a comparable panoply of forms in my own imagination, it would take a lot of effort that I definitely haven't made.) I'm replying as best I can and waiting for developments.

Why am I telling you this? Because if one were to ask for which archetypes of higher beings, traditional or otherwise, for whom these dreams would be a suitable calling card, Shub-Ne'hurrath would be very high on the list. Particularly, if one combines your account of her role in nature with aspects of the Cthulhu Mythos' authors visualization of her form. The dreams, then, relate to some of your insights here (e.g. mapping the mythos monster to the Mother Goddess and the Binah sphere), which sends me looking further in that direction.

I guess my actual question comes down to, is there anything of interest about Shub-Ne'hurrath in your notes or thoughts that didn't make it into the Haliverse novels or onto this blog?

replies.title: Re: Bio Dreams
replies.content: Let's see. The Black Goat's friendlier to humans than most of the Great Old Ones; that's actually from Lovecraft, but it's also true in my novels. She likes everything that lives and reproduces, and she's just as fond of snails, blue-green algae, parasitic worms, you name it, as she is of us.  She can appear in any biological form of any gender -- the Black Goat with a Thousand Young is also the Black Ram with a Thousand Ewes -- and people who spend time around her tend to have children, by her or others, more often than not. The people in Kingsport who worship her in Poseidonian fashion -- think Jenny and the little statue of Tsathoggua -- use black candles, and the oil that goes on the potpourri has musk in it; the potpourri itself contains jasmine, patchouli and orris root. That's what comes to mind just at the moment.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG, I have a question about our family's house. I know you have covered this sort of question a number of times, but as a total newbie to this, I'm not sure how to approach our situation.
My family (spouse and 3 children) has lived in our house since we purchased it about 12 years ago. We have had a fair amount of family strife, serious health issues, financial difficulties, and other kinds of life problems in that time. We are at a juncture where some significant action, and change, has to happen.
Reading this blog has me wondering if there is sort of a "negative energy" in the house that we might have "inherited" when we bought the place that is feeding into our problems.
We know a little about the previous owner: She was in her 40's, single, no children, died of some kind of cancer after living in the house about 8 years. Her father, who lives on the other side of the country and who she was apparently estranged from, inherited the house and was the seller. Seemed a very unpleasant man in our brief dealings with him. We are in a moderate sized town (25,000 pop.) and know a lot of people and have met no one who knew her, not even neighbors. She apparently had some kind of professional job (a lawyer, I think) and a good income. The house is of 1910's vintage, modest, and well built (classic lines and proportions, quality craftsmanship and materials, … you know the kind, I guess). It then had a bunch of very poor quality work done on it by her - "improvements", according to the realtor, that we are still working on "un-improving", if you get my drift.
Before her, the house had been owned by a couple who had been in it since the 1950's, raised a family (haven't found any details on this), were well regarded in the community, and died, apparently, of age related issues.
There is a small, poorly constructed addition that was built for her mother, but the mother died before she could move in. It is such an odd size and shape that we have tried multiple ways of using it but they've all descended into chaos. We're half tempted to just tear it off!  With 5 of us here, we do kinda need the space.
The kitchen had been remodeled, again with poorly thought out arrangement, poor quality materials, inferior workmanship, etc. We are in the process of gutting and redoing it.
The roof began leaking a few years into our tenure and we were unable to get it fixed for a few years after that. There is still some resulting interior water stains and peeling paint that we haven't gotten to fixing. 
There's a long list of other little things that we inherited that hint at a history of lack of regard for the house.
Our first 6 or so years here were dominated by caring for small children and various health and financial problems and we were mainly focused on just cleaning up the place, settling in, and daily living. 
The past 6 years we have begun to address some of the bigger structural issues of the house. The kids need much less moment-to-moment care, the financial and health issues have eased, but the dynamics of family relations and our marriage have deteriorated.
I am an experienced carpenter and have done much of the work myself (except for during a period when I was physically incapacitated and we had some volunteer help). My sense of the "bones" of the house when I've dug into various projects has been extremely positive, most of it's initial charm and grace is still there, and it's in a location that is extremely good for all of us. We do want to fix or heal the house.

So, we have the sense of an unhappy, isolated previous owner who died an untimely death and the sense of a house that was once proud of itself but was mistreated and is now sort of depressed.

My spouse, especially, senses that there is sort of a sickness in the house that is partly from its history prior to us, partly from our bad feeling about how it looks and feels to us and to visitors, and is partly from the negative family dynamics that have developed here. Sort of a vicious circle where the house's bad energy makes us feel (and act) badly and then results in the house feeling even worse.

So I'm wondering where to start on clearing the house of "bad energy".
Stuff I've read about on your blog includes: floor washes, burning some kind of herbs (?), other kinds of folk magic I can't quite remember…
 Maybe some sort of sphere of protection, which I'm intrigued by but haven't done? Or a blessing of some kind? I don't know where to start. I'm not part of any religious tradition.
Is it possible to talk to the house directly? Ask what it needs? Is it injured in some way?
Also, I'm curious if it's just bad energy of some kind, or could something of the previous, deceased owner's spirit or ghost still be lurking. Does this matter? Would our approach differ? How would we figure this out?
(Some of the problems with the house are sort of a feng-shui issue, and we are pursuing that as well.)
I think you can see this is a bigger deal than just being comfortable in our home. It is about healing the growing problems in our marriage and in relationship dynamics with our kids. We are doing a number of other things, which I might ask about later.
For right now I'm asking about the house (and home) aspect of the problem. 
I should note that my spouse has contributed to and approved this request! Just the excercise of formulating some clear questions for you has been beneficial to us.

Any advice on how to proceed would be greatly appreciated.
Thanks
D

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, the house very likely has a lot of problematic energy. Start by buying a bottle of Florida water (a variety of cologne), a new broom and (unless you have wall to wall carpet) a new mop. Sweep the entire house, beginning in the kitchen and ending by sweeping it all out the front door. Then take up any carpets you have, hang them outside to air, and mix up a bucket of mop water that contains 1 cup lemon ammonia, 1/2 cup kosher salt, and 1 tablespoon of the hottest red pepper you can find. Mop the whole house with this, again starting with the kitchen and ending at the front door. Once you've done this, splash a little Florida water in the four corners of every room in the house. That should clear off a lot of the energy; you should do this at least once a year. If you feel up to taking up a daily banishing ritual, that would also help, but don't neglect the basic hoodoo cleaning (which is what I've just described).

You can also get from hoodoo supply stores some light blue candles and Happy Home oil (also known as Peaceful Home oil). Once each week, on a Thursday, anoint the candle with the oil, repeat a request for peace and happiness in your home and let the candle burn all the way down. (Do this in a safe where there's no danger of fire.) That will help fill the house with positive energy to counter the nasty stuff. Give it a try and see what results you get.

Pat in Florida, of a smaller library and authors who, uh, have their quirks.

replies.title: Greek gods
replies.content:
The Homeric Hymns (not by Homer but once attributed to him) give Leto and Zeus as the parents of Apollo and Artemis--who were born on the island of Delos, not at Delphi. Pan is described as son of Hermes and a mortal woman, the daughter of a king Dryops. I can't find any mention of Pan in my copy of the Theogony. Walter Burkert in _Greek Relgion_ sees Pan as god of flocks and generation who is only later connected with an idea of a universal god, but nothing on his ancestry. Somewhere I saw a suggestion that Pan did not come to Athens until the Persian Wars (remember his role in the battle of Marathon). Somehow a chart of the Greek gods seems to have gone walkabout. I also checked to see what Robert Graves had to say but Pan doesn't appear in The White Goddess and I don't own Graves Greek Mythology.

Rita

replies.title: Otherness of Pan
replies.content:
Maybe Pan chiefly came across as Erotic in post-Victorian 1902-1952 Britain; but might he not be expressed differently in other times, places and contexts? Perhaps as hairy coarseness, rank smell and wild woods freedom versus sleek and perfumed conventions of sophisticated urbanity. Natural honesty vs. artifice and trumpery. Or sensual vs. intellectual.

Untameable, inexplicable, unpredictable nature vs. all attempts to reduce the world to military control and scientific rationality. Exasperating! Merriment and pleasure vs. Calvinist hard work and hard-nosed Scots Presbyterianism. Teasing, tempting mischiefmaking. Unruly. Goatlike.

A Wild Thing, big, shining eyes in the dark forest from which you run in primal panic fear. Unless you define the Erotic very loosely to cover all these cases under one umbrella, I would say they are different facets of one complex Being, and relate to more than just the genital center.

As noted many MMs ago, I ran across Steiner's discussion of the need to balance thinking, feeling, and willing capacities. Since one would naturally assume that the passionate self feels and the reasoning self thinks, does that not mean that it is then the vital self which wills?

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) As you read, ask yourself: what would it be like if this were true of the world? What would I be like if this were true of the world?

2) Nope -- the reasoning self consists of will, memory, and thought. The vital body is what keeps you alive; the closest it gets to will is reflex.

comments.title: Christianity and other gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG,


I was surprised and somewhat distressed to read your recent statement here that the promise to "reject Satan and all of his works" in Christian initiation is an implicit rejection of all the gods of other faiths; I've always understood this as a reference to forces like the Qliphoth which are antithetical to Creation or fundamentally hostile to humanity. My own intuition has always been that the Christian God is more tolerant of polytheism than orthodoxy claims, and that He would have no qualms with His followers offering reverence to the powers of nature as long as they reserve true worship for Him. I've been re-engaging with the Catholic Church lately, and although I've never been confirmed there are a few occasions where the whole congregation repeats the baptismal vows; I've had no problem joining in, because of the interpretation I described above.


1. Does the intention of the speaker matter in initiatory statements/vows like this, or is it all based on the theological framework of the religion in question?
2. Would you say that I've already gone past the point where I would suffer blowback if I invoked a Pagan spirit, or would I need to have actually gone through the sacrament of confirmation?
3. I've been practicing a Christian version of the SOP, with the names of Jesus at the various directions. Do you think that continuing to practice magic like this would also be incompatible with Communion and confirmation?


I'm troubled by this, because I've been operating under the tentative hypothesis that I've been drawn toward Catholicism at least partly because Christ thinks my perspective is needed in the Church; I have a half-glimpsed vision of a future Christianity that looks more like Tolkein than Aquinas, with room for Elves and Ents and the hidden powers of the land and sea alongside the Creator and his angels, and I'd like to do play some small part in bringing that vision about. But I don't know if I can accept Communion if it means literally demonizing the gods of every other tradition. I suppose I'll have to pray about it, and hope that I get better at hearing the answers.


Many thanks for reading and answering our endless questions!
-Fred N.

replies.title: Re: Christianity and other gods
replies.content:
Hi Brother Fred N.,
I am of the Methodist tradition, not Catholic, but it seems to me there is a very large difference between spirits of place, of nature, and so on, and anything that might be termed a god, and still greater between they and the works of Satan.

If you have read Gareth Knight's Experience of the Inner World, he quotes a conversation with Pan. He is Episcopalian, so between you and I in the genology of the Church, and sees nothing out of line with such a chat.

We shouldn't worship those spirits. But then, we don't worship saints either, do we? Catholics and Orthodox do offer saints respect and ask for their intervention and thank them for it, and there's nothing inherent in my tradition that forbids that, at least to the best of my research, it's simply fallen out of custom. Thus our cat received the name of the patron saint of cats, and I suspect the little fool's namesake is watching out for her quite diligently! (The cat has even gotten shut up in the oven, but is alive and unharmed.)

I suggest that my Masonic Brother Greer, being not a Christian, has perhaps a different view of Who we follow than we do, and certainly many of our Christian brothers and sisters are stridently exclusive. I suppose they need to be. We should always recollect that others may have their own viewpoints, concerns, and temptations, as described in 1 Corinthians 8, and conduct ourselves accordingly.

As I've never heard from our Father that He objects to my talking to the local nature spirits, and He certainly has told me when I'm out of line in another area, I'm satisfied with my conclusions. You should of course ask Him, as I did. They are part of His creation and subject to Him.

BoysMom

replies.title: GD and New Age
replies.content:
Very late to be commenting, but the description of the Golden Dawn as "new-agey pop-magic" is so bizarre that I thought about it, and came up with some devil's-advocate justifications.

1. The HOGD system was the first, or at least among the first, new systems or approaches to magic to be developed after the planet's entry into the Age of Aquarius. In a technical sense, it is therefore New Age magic.
2. A person had to have at least an upper middle class income to be able to afford the fees for membership in the Order, participation in any of its temples and acquiring the tools. Admission fees for New Age seminars, workshops and retreats are also priced at a level that is only affordable for people in the upper middle class or above.
3. The founders of the HOGD claimed the equivalent of Ascended Masters as the source of their authority and the secret teachings of the Order.
4. The Golden Dawn was a syncretic system, borrowing and reworking source materials from such disparate sources as Hinduism, Ancient Egyptian religion, and Renaissance ceremonial magic. New Age teachers do the same, only even more so.

I can't come up with any justification for describing the Golden Dawn as pop-magic, though. Popular magic is easy to learn and to do, and aims at quick results. That's what makes it popular.
